bins.name: Proactive Planning Over Reactive Study
bins.desc: AEP argues that piecemeal, reactive interconnection studies are insufficient to handle large load growth and that the Commission must drive proactive, multi-value, long-term transmission planning. Short-term flexible tools are useful but cannot substitute for forward-looking network expansion.
quotes: Without much needed reforms, the current approaches threaten to produce insufficient and inefficient infrastructure to reliably support large load growth and economic development.
Short-term, flexible approaches can facilitate speed-to-power, but they cannot ensure a transmission system that can support the projected large load growth.

While short-term speed-to-power solutions are important, the Commission cannot overlook the need to foster proactive transmission planning, which is necessary to ensure cost-effective and affordable transmission system expansion and timely interconnection of new generation resources.

Proactive, multi-value planning is also crucial to foster the development of Extra High Voltage ('EHV') facilities—the essential backbone of the nation's transmission system—because it looks ahead to identify regional and interregional needs and the corresponding EHV solutions.
The Commission should support regional efforts to move towards consolidated, integrated planning, like SPP's CPP.

bins.name: Rolled-in Rates for Network Upgrades
bins.desc: AEP supports rolling large load network upgrade costs into transmission rates rather than directly assigning them to interconnecting customers. This protects existing customers by making large loads pay their fair share as system users, and ensures transmission owners can attract capital for needed infrastructure.
quotes: Without much needed reforms, the current approaches threaten to produce insufficient and inefficient infrastructure to reliably support large load growth and economic development.

To protect other customers, large loads must pay their fair share for the transmission system expansion needed to serve them, and the Commission also needs to fundamentally reconsider how generator interconnection and transmission system planning are conducted to ensure that reforms drive cost-effective development to support interconnecting loads.

AEP agrees with commenters arguing that network upgrade costs for large load interconnections should be rolled into transmission rates and that direct assignment of 100% of such network upgrade costs to large load customers is not appropriate.
This is critical to ensure that transmission owners can continue to attract the capital necessary to develop the transmission infrastructure needed to support the interconnection of large loads.

While short-term speed-to-power solutions are important, the Commission cannot overlook the need to foster proactive transmission planning, which is necessary to ensure cost-effective and affordable transmission system expansion and timely interconnection of new generation resources.

bins.name: Conditional Service as Bridge Tool
bins.desc: AEP endorses offering conditional or non-firm transmission service to large loads and co-located generators as a short-term bridge until full network upgrades are complete. Customers willing to accept curtailment could come online faster, but terms must protect reliability and ensure fair cost payment.
quotes: Temporary conditional service is a short-term speed-to-power solution that can bring new generation and load on line quickly and be used as a bridge service until the transmission system is upgraded to support full network service to those interconnections.
If large loads are willing to accept conditional transmission service and be curtailed, the Commission could adopt rules that allow LSEs to bring large loads online faster.
Short-term, flexible approaches can facilitate speed-to-power, but they cannot ensure a transmission system that can support the projected large load growth.

bins.name: Network Upgrade Cost Treatment
bins.desc: AEP opposes direct assignment of 100% of network upgrade costs to large load customers and favors rolled-in rate treatment consistent with longstanding Commission precedent. Proper cost allocation also ensures transmission owners earn a lawful return to attract investment capital.
quotes: To protect other customers, large loads must pay their fair share for the transmission system expansion needed to serve them, and the Commission also needs to fundamentally reconsider how generator interconnection and transmission system planning are conducted to ensure that reforms drive cost-effective development to support interconnecting loads.

AEP agrees with commenters arguing that network upgrade costs for large load interconnections should be rolled into transmission rates and that direct assignment of 100% of such network upgrade costs to large load customers is not appropriate.
This is critical to ensure that transmission owners can continue to attract the capital necessary to develop the transmission infrastructure needed to support the interconnection of large loads.

Proactive, multi-value planning is also crucial to foster the development of Extra High Voltage ('EHV') facilities—the essential backbone of the nation's transmission system—because it looks ahead to identify regional and interregional needs and the corresponding EHV solutions.

bins.name: Speed-to-Power Tools and Pathways
bins.desc: AEP supports exploring expedited interconnection tools such as conditional service for curtailable loads, advanced conductor reconductoring, and consolidated planning processes that shorten study timelines. These should complement, not replace, longer-term infrastructure solutions.
quotes: Temporary conditional service is a short-term speed-to-power solution that can bring new generation and load on line quickly and be used as a bridge service until the transmission system is upgraded to support full network service to those interconnections.
If large loads are willing to accept conditional transmission service and be curtailed, the Commission could adopt rules that allow LSEs to bring large loads online faster.
Reconductoring with advanced conductors can greatly increase capacity in a short time frame (less than two years), utilizing existing towers and rights-of-way, thereby avoiding siting processes.

bins.name: SPP as Planning Model
bins.desc: AEP highlights SPP's HILL, HILLGA, and Consolidated Planning Process (CPP) as promising models the Commission should encourage other regions to adopt. These proposals shorten study time and provide generators with upfront cost certainty through an entry-fee approach.
quotes: The Commission should support regional efforts to move towards consolidated, integrated planning, like SPP's CPP.
The Commission's reforms should avoid introducing additional delays to large load interconnection by disrupting state and regional-level efforts.

bins.name: Preserve PJM Stakeholder Progress
bins.desc: AEP notes that PJM has an active stakeholder process to develop large load interconnection reforms and urges the Commission to avoid disrupting that work with prescriptive federal requirements.
quotes: The Commission's reforms should avoid introducing additional delays to large load interconnection by disrupting state and regional-level efforts.

bins.name: Preserve MISO Regional Processes
bins.desc: AEP groups MISO alongside other regions where ongoing state and RTO-level efforts are underway, arguing federal reforms should be additive rather than preemptive.
quotes: The Commission's reforms should avoid introducing additional delays to large load interconnection by disrupting state and regional-level efforts.

bins.name: Principles-Based Regional Flexibility
bins.desc: AEP argues that Commission reforms should be principles-based and avoid prescriptive one-size-fits-all requirements, preserving the ability of states and regions to tailor solutions to their specific large load growth needs.
quotes: Any rules adopted by the Commission in this proceeding should be principles-based, avoid prescriptive requirements, and allow for regional flexibility.
The Commission's reforms should avoid introducing additional delays to large load interconnection by disrupting state and regional-level efforts.

bins.name: State Tariff Success as Template
bins.desc: AEP points to its own data center tariffs in Ohio and other states, which attracted over 13,000 MW of applications within months, as evidence that state-level solutions are working and should not be disrupted by federal action.
quotes: Since implementation of the Tariff on July 11, 2025, '36 data center cites representing over 13,000 MW of new load have filed applications' to interconnect.

bins.name: Advanced Conductors for Fast Capacity Gains
bins.desc: AEP supports Commission incentives for reconductoring with advanced conductors, which can increase line capacity in under two years using existing towers and rights-of-way. AEP has a $1.6 billion DOE loan guarantee to reconductor roughly 5,000 miles across five states.
quotes: Reconductoring with advanced conductors can greatly increase capacity in a short time frame (less than two years), utilizing existing towers and rights-of-way, thereby avoiding siting processes.
